title: The Long Game
author: Jordan L. Shlain MD
content_chunks: Twenty-five years from vision to vindication Press enter or click to view image in full size Let me tell you how I got here. Not with a PowerPoint deck or a business plan, but with something deeper — a peculiar inheritance from my parents that shaped how I see the world and, ultimately, how I would rebuild a broken piece of it. My father was a surgeon. Detroit-born, Midwestern values to his core, deeply dedicated and loyal to his profession. Surgery is beautifully straightforward in its own way: you identify the problem, you cut something out or fix something, and then you move on to the next patient. It’s transactional, tangible, and immediately rewarding. You can see what you’ve accomplished. My mother took a different path. She earned her PhD in psychology, writing her thesis on building long-term relationships and the power of truly talking to people — not at them, but with them. Her work was about connection, understanding, patience, and the slow accumulation of trust. Somehow, I absorbed both. I became someone who wanted to build deep relationships while also being a pragmatic, practical, expeditious problem solver. I wanted the meaningful connection of my mother’s world and the decisive action of my father’s. Most people in medicine choose one lane or the other. I refused to. This hybrid thinking would become the foundation of everything I built. The Question That Changed Everything It’s the late 1990s. I’m sitting across from Dr. Gary Mecklenburg, CEO of Northwestern Hospital, pitching him on workplace clinics — medical facilities built right inside companies to save employees the hassle of leaving work to see their doctor. I’m excited, convinced this is revolutionary. Then Gary leans back in the wood-paneled boardroom of Northwestern and asks me a question that would reverberate for the next quarter-century: “Jordan, do you want to make money, or do you want to change the healthcare system?” I felt trapped. The way he framed it, you could only choose one. After a long silence, I told him I’d answer only if he explained why he’d posed such an impossible choice. He agreed — but I had to go first. “I want to change healthcare,” I said. “That’s my reason for being.” Gary nodded, then laid it out like my father might have described a surgical procedure: clear, precise, unsparing. If I built on-site clinics, I could raise serious capital, scale rapidly, sell at the peak, and walk away wealthy. “But you won’t have changed healthcare at all,” he said. “Not even a little bit.” The alternative required something else entirely — something my mother would have understood: “If you want to change healthcare, build one clinic. Refine it. Grow it carefully. Build more. Learn organically. And after twenty years, you might — might — be able to change healthcare.”

I chose the long game. Not because I’m some kind of saint, but because those Midwestern values wouldn’t let me sleep at night if I took the quick money and ran. I had seen something broken, and I couldn’t unsee it. HMOs running amok and a gathering army of hospital and insurance administrators with dollar signs in their eyes. By 2002, after years of deep reflection on how to design a system that genuinely served both doctors and patients, I launched what would eventually become Private Medical. What started as “innovation by irritation” — sheer frustration with what people called the healthcare “system” (though it was barely worthy of the name) — led me down a path of exploring what an actual system might look like if it honored doctors and patients equally. When I announced I was leaving the insurance world, the response was swift and brutal. Colleagues mocked me. Friends questioned my judgment. I was accused of abandoning those who needed care most, of selling out, of elitism. The vitriol was surprising in its intensity. But I had history on my side, and I wasn’t afraid to deploy it. I reminded the older physicians — the ones most critical of what Private Medical represented — of something they seemed to have forgotten: “You didn’t always take insurance. There was a time when patients paid you directly. Remember?” “What happened,” I’d continue, “is that you were divided and conquered. The system didn’t just change how you got paid. It changed what you were called. You went from being doctors — healers with autonomy and authority — to providers, interchangeable parts in a machine designed to process human beings like claims forms.” Some got angry. Some got quiet. A few got curious enough to listen. The entire “concierge medicine” field is the byproduct of a market failure. That market failure stems from the system’s fundamental belief that specialty care was more valuable than primary care. They put primary care in the corner — underfunded it, devalued it, burdened it with impossible patient loads and soul-crushing documentation requirements. And now we are leaving in droves. Welcome to the emergent property of this market failure. The very early days at Private Medical I kept building Private Medical. Not because I was certain of success, but because I was certain of the problem. People were starving for humanity in medicine — they wanted their doctor to actually know them, to have time for them, to care about them as whole human beings rather than as a parade of symptoms to be coded and billed. While my critics remained captive to a system they claimed was the only viable option, I was experiencing something they’d forgotten was possible: the deep satisfaction of practicing medicine the way it was meant to be practiced. With my father’s pragmatism and my mother’s relationship-building, Private Medical became a place where we could solve problems decisively while maintaining genuine long-term connections with our patients.

I brought in other physicians who shared this mission — doctors who weren’t afraid of accountability, who embraced transparency, who understood that you can’t truly heal people when you’re rushing them through seven-minute appointments optimized for billing codes rather than outcomes. Me, Rex Chiu MD and Eric Swagel MD — early days of Private Medical But let me be honest about what these twenty-five years have actually been like. Get Jordan L. Shlain MD’s stories in your inbox Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer. There have been extraordinary highs. Catching a cancer early because we had time for a thorough exam. Preventing a heart attack because we knew our patient well enough to recognize subtle changes. Saving lives because the relationship we’d built meant patients actually told us what was really happening, not just what they thought we wanted to hear. And there have been devastating lows. Losing patients despite our best efforts. Watching families grieve. Facing the humbling reality that even with time, attention, and genuine care, medicine has limits. Death doesn’t negotiate. The headwinds were constant. The haters were loud. Building something truly unique meant enduring years of people telling you that you were wrong, naive, and destined to fail. Some days, I wondered if they were right. But I stayed anchored to what I call the “problem surface” of medicine — primary care. This is where the system breaks down first and most catastrophically. This is where patients lose access, where doctors burn out, where the promise of healthcare collides with the grinding reality of a system optimized for everything except actual care. Private Medical grew slowly, organically — just as Gary Mecklenburg predicted. We refined our model. We learned what worked and what didn’t. We added physicians in new cities, each time ensuring they understood that this wasn’t just a different payment structure; it was a unique philosophy of care. Through it all, I kept returning to a fundamental truth: medicine is a calling. It’s something that feeds the soul when done right, and something that hollows you out when the system forces you to practice in ways that betray everything you believed when you took your oath. My father felt this about surgery. My mother felt this about the therapeutic relationship. I felt it about primary care. We need to rebuild a system in which we can all feel good about the field of medicine again. Vindication and the Path Forward Twenty-five years later — Gary was off by five years, but close enough — the New England Journal of Medicine published “The Concierge Cure?” It wasn’t published as a curiosity or a controversial outlier, but as a serious analysis of a phenomenon they could no longer ignore. Reading it felt surreal. Like watching archaeologists excavate something you built with your own hands and hearing them marvel at its construction.

The article acknowledges what I knew in 2002: traditional primary care is “perpetually near collapse.” It recognizes that the system’s failure to support the care-quarterbacking that primary care doctors provide has real consequences for patients. It grapples with the uncomfortable question I forced into the open two and a half decades ago: What if the system itself is the problem? They reference Private Medical specifically — our founder story, our philosophy, our approach. They quote Eric Swagel, who joined Private Medical as cofounder in 2009, explaining how traditional models “punish dedicated doctors” while concierge practices realign incentives so that “what’s good for the doctor is good for the patient.” The article describes me as the originator, the vision, the godfather of concierge medicine. Building something truly unique meant enduring years of people telling you that you were wrong, naive, and destined to fail. But here’s what the New England Journal of Medicine article really represents: acknowledgment that we are at an important moment. We need to discuss openly the critical role of primary care in improving outcomes and, ultimately, controlling costs. The foundation of good healthcare isn’t the specialist who performs the procedure — it’s the primary care physician who knows you well enough to determine whether you need that procedure in the first place. Yan Chin MD, Sanjay Banker, Eric Swagel MD, me 2025 The article doesn’t just validate concierge medicine. It indicts the system that made it necessary. It acknowledges that we’ve created a healthcare infrastructure that penalizes devoted doctors and fails patients, all while burning through resources at an unsustainable rate. Concierge medicine works. Private Medical works. We’ve proven that you can create financial sustainability while maintaining professional integrity. We’ve demonstrated that you can thrive outside the insurance industrial complex. We’ve shown that both doctors and patients will choose this model when given the chance. But we’re not the complete solution — we’re a proof of concept. We serve a fraction of the population, and I harbor no illusions that membership-based medicine alone will solve healthcare’s crisis. What Private Medical and the broader concierge medicine field show is that different models are possible, that quality and sustainability can coexist, that we don’t have to accept perpetual collapse as the price of medical care. The real victory isn’t the New England Journal of Medicine article, though I won’t pretend it doesn’t feel validating after twenty-five years of criticism. The real victory is the millions of patients who have a doctor again — who can reach their physician, who receive unhurried care, who experience medicine as a relationship rather than a transaction. There are over 10,000 concierge practices in the US, and now doctors from all over the world, including Canada, the UK and France, are calling me to show them a better way.

The real victory is the physicians at Private Medical and practices like ours who remember why they went to medical school, who go home at night feeling like they actually helped people, who aren’t drowning in documentation and bureaucracy designed to serve everyone except the patient. The real victory is proving that you can opt out of a broken system and build something better, even when everyone tells you it’s impossible. Private Medical team off-site 2025 My father taught me to solve problems decisively and move forward. My mother taught me that real healing happens in relationships, built over time. I combined both approaches to create Private Medical — a practice model that delivers pragmatic solutions within enduring relationships. The New England Journal of Medicine asks the right question at the end: “If everyone deserves to have a doctor, how can we create the conditions to meet this need?” That’s the question I’ve been asking for twenty-five years. I answered it one way. Others will answer it differently. But the important thing is that we’re finally asking it openly, seriously, with the recognition that our current trajectory is unsustainable. Gary Mecklenburg was right: you can make money quickly, or you can change healthcare slowly. Twenty-five years later, the New England Journal of Medicine is taking notes. They’re acknowledging that what seemed like a boutique service for the wealthy is actually a warning signal — a canary in the coal mine showing us what happens when you systematically undervalue the most important layer of medical care. Primary care doctors are leaving traditional practice in droves. Not because we’re greedy. Not because we don’t care. But because the system has made it nearly impossible to practice medicine in a way that feeds the soul rather than depleting it. The long game is lonely sometimes. There were nights I questioned everything, moments when the criticism cut deep, periods when growth was slow and the path unclear. There were patients we saved and patients we lost. There were triumphs and heartbreaks. That’s medicine. That’s what it means for this work to be a calling rather than just a career. But it turns out the long game is also how you change the world — not in spite of the headwinds, but through them. Not by following the path everyone else took, but by clearing a new one. Not by accepting what was, but by building what could be. Thank you to the New England Journal of Medicine Group for acknowledging we are at a critical juncture. The conversation about primary care’s role in healthcare outcomes can no longer be avoided. The market failure is too obvious. The exodus too pronounced. The need too urgent. Private Medical is not the only answer. But we are an answer. We are proof that another way is possible. And after twenty-five years of swimming against the current, it’s deeply gratifying to see the current beginning to shift. 2025 Prviate Medical team off-site

That’s how I got here. With my father’s surgical precision and my mother’s commitment to relationship. With innovation born of irritation. With the stubborn belief that if everyone deserves to have a doctor, then someone needed to build a system that made that possible. The work continues. The calling persists. The soul remains fed. And finally, finally, the field is beginning to pay attention. Family photo with my parents and sisters Purveyor of subtleties in the science of medicine. Inspired by phenomena. Ideas are fuel. Twenty-five years is a timeframe that makes most conventional startup wisdom irrelevant. The frameworks designed for 3–7 year venture cycles — lean methodology, product-market fit sprints, growth hacking — assume a world where your core thesis can…more The contrast between your father’s decisiveness and your mother’s long-term relational thinking really stood out. It feels like most systems today over-optimize for one and ignore the other, which is probably why they break over time. The surgeon analogy cuts right to it — medicine trains you to find the problem, fix it, move on. But rebuilding something systemic means sitting with ambiguity for years, sometimes decades, without the feedback loop that surgery gives you. That…more

author_profile.bio: Purveyor of subtleties in the science of medicine. Inspired by phenomena. Ideas are fuel.

title: The Art of Receiving Feedback: How to Stay Connected When You’re Being Criticized
author: Malynnda Stewart, PhD, BCPA
content_chunks: The Art of Receiving Feedback: How to Stay Connected When You’re Being Criticized Press enter or click to view image in full size Feedback Faces (upset, neutral, happy) I was in a meeting with my boss when I first noticed it. She was giving me feedback about a presentation I’d delivered, nothing harsh, nothing cruel, just observations about what could have been stronger. But as she spoke, I felt her words hit me. Not metaphorically. Actually, physically hit me, right in the center of my chest. My face got hot. My throat tightened. I could feel my heart rate pick up. And suddenly I wasn’t really hearing what she was saying anymore. I was just managing the physical sensation of being under attack while trying to look as if I were calmly receiving professional feedback. This was years ago, when I was just beginning to learn about how our bodies respond to words, about nervous system activation and threat response. And in that moment, I had this clarifying thought: My body thinks I’m being attacked. Even though I’m not. Even though this is just feedback. My nervous system can’t tell the difference. I’ve thought about that moment a lot since then. Because I wasn’t unique in that response. I see it constantly in my work as a mediator and communication trainer: people whose bodies respond to feedback as if it’s a physical threat, even when the feedback is delivered kindly, even when it’s accurate, even when they intellectually know it’s meant to help. The problem isn’t the feedback itself. The problem is what happens in our nervous systems when we receive it. And until we understand that, until we learn to work with our bodies instead of against them, we can’t actually hear feedback. We can only survive it. What Actually Happens When We Hear Criticism Over the years, I’ve learned more about what was happening in my body during that meeting with my boss. Research shows that when we receive feedback, especially feedback suggesting we did something wrong or could have done better, our nervous system scans for threat. For many of us, particularly those of us who experienced criticism as children that felt like rejection, or who work in environments where feedback has been weaponized, our nervous systems interpret feedback as a threat. Not “this behavior needs adjustment” danger. But “you are fundamentally flawed and your worth is in question” danger. Recent neuroimaging research by Cheng and Berman (2022) demonstrates that receiving criticism activates the same neural threat networks as physical pain. Our amygdala, the threat-detection center of our brain, can’t distinguish between “your presentation needed stronger data” and “you are inadequate.” It just knows: criticism detected, threat response activated.

So our body does what bodies do when they detect a threat: it prepares us to fight, flee, or freeze. Blood flow shifts away from our prefrontal cortex, the part that does rational thinking and perspective-taking, toward our survival systems. Our heart rate increases. Our muscles tense. Our thinking narrows to immediate threat management. Neuroscientist Stephen Porges’s updated polyvagal theory (2021) helps explain this process. Our autonomic nervous system is constantly evaluating safety through what Porges calls “neuroception”, a subconscious detection of threat that happens before conscious awareness. When feedback triggers this threat detection, our body automatically shifts into a defensive state, even when we intellectually know we’re not in danger. And in that state, we leave our window of tolerance. Remember, the window of tolerance is the zone where we can process information, regulate emotions, and respond thoughtfully. Outside that window, we flip into hyperarousal or hypoarousal, and neither state can actually receive feedback. In hyperarousal, we get defensive. We explain. We justify. We counter with our own feedback about them. We need them to understand why what they’re saying isn’t quite right, isn’t the whole story, isn’t fair. The pressure to defend ourselves feels urgent, necessary, as if we don’t explain right now, they’ll walk away with the wrong impression of us. In hypoarousal, we shut down. We go quiet. We nod along, but we’re not really hearing anything — we’re just waiting for it to be over. We might say “okay” or “I understand,” but inside, we’ve disconnected, gone numb, retreated to somewhere far away where their words can’t reach us. Either way, we’re not actually receiving the feedback. We’re just managing our nervous system’s response to perceived threat. What makes this so challenging is that the feedback doesn’t have to be harsh or cruel or poorly delivered for this to happen. Research by Motro, Spoelma, and Ellis (2021) found that even mild negative feedback significantly impairs cognitive flexibility and processing, the very capacities we need to learn from feedback. Even feedback given with care and good intentions can trigger this response if our nervous system is primed to interpret criticism as a threat. The False Urgency of Defending Yourself I’ve written before about false urgency when giving feedback, that pressure to solve it right now, that’s actually just our own discomfort disguised as necessity. But false urgency also shows up when we’re receiving feedback, and it’s just as destructive. It shows up as the immediate pressure to defend yourself. To explain your reasoning, your context, and your intentions. To make sure they understand why you did what you did, that you’re not actually the person they seem to be describing, that there’s more to the story.

That pressure feels urgent. It feels like if you don’t explain right now, this second, they’ll walk away thinking something about you that isn’t true. They’ll form an opinion you won’t be able to change. They’ll see you as incompetent, careless, or unprofessional. But that urgency is almost never real. It’s your nervous system’s threat response, trying to make the discomfort stop as fast as possible. False urgency also manifests as a need to fix it immediately. To promise you’ll do better, to outline your plan for improvement, to demonstrate right now in this conversation that you’re taking it seriously and won’t make this mistake again. You feel like you can’t just sit with the feedback. You have to show them you’re responding to it, addressing it, solving it. Because maybe if you fix it fast enough, the uncomfortable feeling of being criticized will go away. False urgency also shows up as the impulse to turn it around. To deflect by offering feedback about them, or about the situation, or about all the things that are actually working well that they didn’t mention. Not because those things aren’t true, but because your nervous system is trying to even the score, to restore your sense of adequacy by proving you’re not just the person who needs feedback — you’re also the person who can give it. All of these impulses feel necessary in the moment. They feel like appropriate, immediate responses to feedback. But they’re actually your nervous system trying to escape a threat as quickly as possible. When you act on these impulses, when you immediately defend, explain, fix, or deflect, you prevent yourself from actually receiving the feedback. You’re so busy managing your threat response that you can’t hear what’s actually being said. I learned this the hard way in that meeting with my boss. As she was giving me feedback, I was already formulating my explanation. Planning what I’d say to contextualize my choices, to show her my reasoning, to demonstrate that I wasn’t just careless or unprepared. Because I was doing that, I missed half of what she actually said. I was so focused on defending myself that I couldn’t hear her. Which meant I left that meeting with an incomplete understanding of her feedback and a missed opportunity to actually learn something. The false urgency protected me from the full impact of the discomfort. But it also protected me from the value of the feedback itself. The Confusion Between Behavior and Identity

Here’s what I think is at the core of why feedback feels so threatening: we struggle to separate criticism of our behavior from criticism of our identity. When someone says, “This presentation needed stronger data,” our nervous system often hears, “You are not smart enough.” When someone says, “The way you handled that situation created problems,” we hear, “You are a problem.” When someone says “this work doesn’t meet the standard,” we hear “you are not good enough.” We take feedback about what we did and make it mean something about who we are. This isn’t irrational. Research on stress appraisal by Jamieson and colleagues (2023) demonstrates that how we interpret feedback, as a threat to our identity versus a challenge to improve , fundamentally changes our physiological response. When we appraise feedback as an identity threat, our bodies respond with the stress profile associated with danger: increased cortisol, narrowed attention, and reduced cognitive flexibility. When we can appraise it as information about behavior, our stress response is moderated, and learning becomes possible. For many of us, this conflation was learned early. Maybe we had parents or teachers who didn’t distinguish between “you made a mistake” and “you are a mistake.” Maybe we grew up in environments where our worth felt conditional on our performance. Maybe we’ve had bosses or partners who weaponized feedback, using criticism of behavior as a way to attack character. So our nervous systems learned: feedback equals threat to identity. Criticism equals rejection. “You could improve” equals “you are inadequate.” Now, even when someone is carefully giving us feedback about a specific behavior in a specific situation, our nervous system translates it into an indictment of our fundamental worth. As I reflect on many of my mediation cases, this becomes clear. Someone receives feedback like “when you interrupted me in the meeting, I felt like my input wasn’t valued.” And they hear it as “you are a bad person who doesn’t respect others.” The feedback giver meant: this specific behavior in this specific moment had this impact. The feedback receiver heard: this is who you are, fundamentally, and it’s not good. Then they defend against the identity attack that wasn’t actually happening, which makes the feedback giver feel like they can’t be honest, which damages the relationship, which makes future feedback even harder. Learning to distinguish between feedback about behavior and an attack on identity has been one of the most important practices in my own growth. Because once I could hear “this presentation needed stronger data” as just that, information about one presentation, not a verdict on my intelligence or worth, I could actually use the feedback to improve instead of just defending against it. What Receiving Feedback Does to Your Window of Tolerance If you’ve read my article about the window of tolerance, you know that concept from trauma therapy: the zone where your nervous system can handle stress effectively, think clearly, and stay present.

When you receive feedback, especially feedback that touches on something you care about or feel vulnerable about, you’re at high risk of leaving your window. And once you’re outside your window, you literally cannot receive feedback well. Your brain isn’t in the state required for learning, for processing complex information, for holding multiple perspectives simultaneously. When you’ve gone into hyperarousal (above your window), you become reactive and defensive. Your thinking narrows. Everything becomes about protecting yourself from perceived threat. You can’t really hear what’s being said because you’re too busy preparing your counter-argument, your explanation, your defense. I notice this in my body as that hit-in-the-chest feeling I described earlier. My heart races. My face gets hot. I can feel my jaw clench. My thoughts speed up, jumping immediately to “but here’s why” and “they don’t understand that” and “I need to explain.” When I’m in that state, I’m not really in the conversation anymore. I’m in my head, managing my own activation, trying to get my nervous system to settle while also appearing calm and receptive. And the feedback is just background noise as I fight this internal battle. When you’ve dropped into hypoarousal (below your window), you go numb and disconnected. You might look like you’re listening, but you’re not really there. The feedback washes over you without landing. You nod, you say “okay,” but nothing is actually being received because you’ve dissociated from the discomfort. I’ve noticed this response in myself, too, particularly with feedback from people I really admire or care about. When the stakes feel too high, when disappointing them feels unbearable, sometimes my nervous system just… exits. I’m sitting there, I’m looking at them, but I’ve gone somewhere else inside myself where their words can’t hurt me. Again, in that state, I cannot actually receive the feedback. I’m protected from the full pain of it, but I’m also protected from any value it might have. The work of receiving feedback well isn’t about toughing it out or developing thick skin. It’s about learning to recognize when you’re leaving your window and practicing to stay inside it or come back to it, so you can actually hear what’s being said. How to Actually Receive Feedback So what does it look like to receive feedback without destroying you or the relationship? How do you stay regulated enough to actually hear what’s being said? I’ve learned some practices over the years, not because I’ve mastered this, I haven’t, but because I’ve failed at it enough times to recognize what helps. Notice When You’re Leaving Your Window The first practice is simply awareness. Learning to recognize the physical signs that you’re heading outside your window when receiving feedback. For me, it’s that hit-in-the-chest feeling. The heat in my face. The impulse to immediately explain. The way my thoughts start racing ahead to my defense before the other person has even finished talking.

This might be different for you; maybe your throat tightens. Maybe you feel yourself getting cold and distant. Maybe your stomach drops or your hands start shaking, or you suddenly can’t think straight. Whatever your signs are, learn them. Because once you can recognize “oh, I’m leaving my window right now,” you can choose to do something about it instead of just reacting from that dysregulated state. I’ve started pausing when I notice these signs and saying something like: “I’m noticing I’m having a strong reaction to this. Can we slow down for a second?” That pause, that acknowledgment that I need a moment, has saved me from so many defensive responses I would have regretted. Practice the Pause Before Responding This is maybe the hardest practice, because every fiber of your being, when you’re activated, wants to respond right now. To explain, to defend, to make sure they understand. That immediate response almost never serves you well. Because you’re responding from threat mode, not from your thinking brain. Thus, I’ve learned to build in a pause. Sometimes it’s just taking a breath before I speak. Sometimes it’s saying, “I need a minute to think about what you’re saying.” Sometimes it’s asking if we can continue the conversation later, after I’ve had time to process. The pause isn’t avoidance. It’s creating space for your nervous system to settle enough that you can actually hear and respond thoughtfully instead of reactively. When my boss gave me that feedback about my presentation, I wish I had paused. I wish I had said, “I’m taking this in. Can I think about this and follow up with you tomorrow?” instead of immediately launching into explanations that came from my defensive, activated state. The pause allows your prefrontal cortex to come back online. It lets you move from “how do I defend against this threat” to “what is actually being said here and what can I learn from it?” Ask Clarifying Questions Instead of Defending When you feel that urge to explain or justify, try asking a question instead. Not a defensive question like “well, what about when I…” or “don’t you think that…” But a genuine, curious question aimed at a deeper understanding of their experience. Questions like: “Can you give me a specific example of when that happened?” or “What would you have wanted to see instead?” or “Help me understand the impact that had on you.” This approach aligns with recent research by Kluger and Itzchakov (2022) on the power of listening in workplace interactions. They found that high-quality listening, characterized by genuine curiosity and attention, creates what they call “listening circles” where both parties feel safer and more able to engage with difficult topics. When you ask clarifying questions, you’re not just gathering information; you’re signaling to the other person’s nervous system that you’re engaged and receptive, which helps both of you stay regulated.

This does two things. First, it slows your nervous system’s threat response by shifting you into a state of curiosity rather than defense. Second, it actually helps you receive the feedback more fully because you’re gathering information rather than deflecting it. I’ve noticed that when I can ask genuine questions, my body starts to settle. The defensiveness loosens. I’m not fighting anymore, I’m learning. And that shift from fighting to learning changes everything about how I receive feedback. Distinguish the Behavior from Your Identity The one practice I find myself returning to over and over is actively separating what you did from who you are. When someone gives you feedback, practice hearing it as information about one behavior in one situation, not as a verdict on your character or worth. Get Malynnda Stewart, PhD, BCPA’s stories in your inbox Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer. They said, “This presentation needed stronger data.” They did not say “you are not intelligent.” They said, “The way you handled that situation created problems.” They did not say “you are a problem person.” They said, “This work doesn’t meet the standard.” They did not say “you are inadequate.” I’ve learned to literally do this translation in my head. When I hear feedback, I pause and ask myself: What are they actually saying about my behavior? And what am I adding to that about my identity? Usually, there’s a gap. Usually, they’re talking about something specific, and I’m making it mean something global about who I am. Catching that gap, recognizing where the feedback ends and my interpretation begins, helps me stay regulated enough to actually hear the feedback for what it is instead of defending against what I’ve made it mean. Not all feedback needs to be fully processed and responded to in the moment it’s given. Sometimes, the most skilled thing you can do is recognize you’re too dysregulated to receive it well right now and ask for time. This isn’t avoidance. It’s wisdom about your nervous system’s capacity. If you notice you’re shutting down, if you feel yourself going numb or dissociating, that’s your cue to ask for time. If you notice you’re getting increasingly defensive and you can feel yourself about to say something you’ll regret, that’s your cue to ask for time. It can sound like: “I hear what you’re saying, and I want to take it seriously. I’m noticing I’m having a strong reaction, and I don’t think I can process this well right now. Can we continue this conversation tomorrow?” Or: “I appreciate you sharing this feedback. I need some time to sit with it before I respond. Can I follow up with you later today?”

The key is to actually follow up. Asking for time isn’t a way to avoid the feedback; it’s a way to create conditions where you can actually receive it. I’ve had to learn that asking for time isn’t weak or avoidant. It’s recognizing that my nervous system needs to regulate before I can engage with difficult information. And that recognition is actually a sign of growth, not limitation. Practice Receiving Without Fixing Immediately One thing I admit I’m still learning: you don’t have to solve it right away when you receive the feedback. When someone gives you feedback, you don’t need to immediately outline your improvement plan or promise how you’ll do better or demonstrate that you’re taking it seriously by having solutions ready. You can just… receive it. Say “thank you for telling me this,” or “I hear you,” or “I’m going to think about this.” And then actually think about it. Later. When you’re regulated. The impulse to fix it immediately is often false urgency. It’s your nervous system trying to make the discomfort stop by showing you’re already addressing the problem. But that immediate fixing often means you’re not really digesting the feedback, you’re just managing your own discomfort about receiving it. Some of the most valuable feedback I’ve received has been feedback I just sat with for days before responding. I let it settle. I let my nervous system calm down. I let myself notice what was actually true in what they said versus what was my defensive interpretation. And then, from that more settled place, I could see what needed to change and how I wanted to respond. Receiving without immediately fixing is an act of trust, trust that you don’t need to prove yourself in this moment, that your worth isn’t on trial, that you can sit with the discomfort of being imperfect without it meaning you’re fundamentally flawed.

Learning to receive feedback without going into threat mode, without conflating behavior with identity, without false urgency to defend or fix immediately, this changes everything. It changes your relationships. When people know they can give you feedback without you getting defensive or shutting down, they’re more honest with you. They tell you things earlier, when they’re still small and manageable. They trust you with hard truths because they’ve learned you can handle them. Research by Yeomans and colleagues (2020) on conversational receptiveness found that people who demonstrate openness to opposing views, even when those views challenge them, create conditions where others feel safer being honest, leading to more productive dialogue and stronger relationships. It changes your growth. When you can actually hear feedback instead of just defending against it, you have access to information about yourself that you can’t see on your own. You learn faster. You improve more quickly. You develop genuine self-awareness instead of just a carefully constructed self-image. It changes your work. When you’re not spending all your energy managing your defensive reactions to feedback, you can focus that energy on actually getting better at what you do. You become someone who’s coachable, adaptable, and genuinely open to growth. Finally, it changes your relationship with yourself. When you can separate feedback about your behavior from your fundamental worth, when you can hear “you could improve in this area” without it triggering “I am fundamentally inadequate,” you develop a more stable sense of self. Your worth no longer depends on perfect performance. You can be imperfect without it being catastrophic. Neuroscientist Shir Atzil and colleagues (2022) describe how our social brains develop through ongoing interactions that either confirm or challenge our sense of safety. When we can receive feedback without it threatening our core sense of self, we’re engaging what they call “social homeostasis”, the capacity to maintain our equilibrium even when receiving challenging information from others. This capacity, they argue, is foundational to psychological resilience and healthy relationships. I think back to that meeting with my boss, to that physical sensation of her words hitting me in the chest, and I recognize now what was happening: my nervous system couldn’t tell the difference between “this presentation needed work” and “you are not good enough to be here.” I see now that those are profoundly different things. And learning to distinguish between them, in my body, not just my mind , has made it possible for me to actually receive feedback as the gift it’s meant to be instead of experiencing it as an attack. A Practice for the Next Time Someone Gives You Feedback The next time you’re receiving feedback and you feel that familiar tension, that heat, that impulse to defend or explain or fix immediately, I want you to try something: Pause. Just for a moment. Take one full breath. Notice what’s happening in your body. Where do you feel it? Your chest? Your throat? Your stomach? Just notice without judgment.

Ask yourself: Am I still inside my window of tolerance, or have I left it? If you’ve left it, if you’re activated or shut down, acknowledge that. You might even say it out loud: “I’m noticing I’m having a strong reaction to this.” Then decide: Can I regulate myself enough to stay in this conversation? Or do I need to ask for time? If you can stay, practice one of the skills we talked about: Ask a clarifying question. Remind yourself this is about behavior, not identity. Focus on receiving instead of immediately fixing. If you need time, ask for it. “I want to take this seriously. Can I think about this and get back to you?” And then, this is important, be gentle with yourself about whatever happened in that moment. If you got defensive, shut down, or couldn’t hear it well, that doesn’t make you a failure at receiving feedback. It makes you human, with a nervous system that’s trying to protect you. The work isn’t becoming someone who never has a threat response to feedback. The work is learning to recognize that response and work with it instead of being controlled by it. You’re learning. That’s all any of us are doing. Learning to receive difficult information without it destroying us. Learning to stay connected to ourselves and others, even when we’re being told we could do better. That learning is happening one piece of feedback at a time, one pause at a time, one moment of choosing curiosity over defense at a time. You’re doing better than you think. Even the fact that you’re reading this, thinking about how to receive feedback more skillfully, means you’re doing the work. Keep going. It gets easier. Not perfect, but easier. The relationship with yourself and others becomes possible when you can receive feedback well. They’re worth every uncomfortable moment of practice. Atzil, S., Gao, W., Fradkin, I., & Barrett, L. F. (2022). Growing a social brain. Nature Human Behaviour, 6(11), 1548–1558. Cheng, J. T., & Berman, M. G. (2022). Social feedback and the brain: Neural correlates of receiving praise and criticism. Social Cognitive and Affective Neuroscience, 17(3), 320–332. Jamieson, J. P., Crum, A. J., Goyer, J. P., Marotta, M. E., & Akinola, M. (2023). Optimizing stress responses with reappraisal and mindset interventions: An integrated model. Anxiety, Stress, & Coping, 36(3), 245–261. Kluger, A. N., & Itzchakov, G. (2022). The power of listening at work. Annual Review of Organizational Psychology and Organizational Behavior, 9, 121–146. Motro, D., Spoelma, T. M., & Ellis, A. P. (2021). Incivility and creativity: The role of affect and cognitive flexibility. Journal of Applied Psychology, 106(11), 1683–1696. Porges, S. W. (2021). Polyvagal theory: A biobehavioral journey to sociality. Comprehensive Psychoneuroendocrinology, 7, 100069. Yeomans, M., Minson, J., Collins, H., Chen, F., & Gino, F. (2020). Conversational receptiveness: Improving engagement with opposing views. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 160, 131–148. Thanks for the Feedback by Douglas Stone and Sheila Heen

Difficult Conversations by Douglas Stone, Bruce Patton, and Sheila Heen On Nervous System and Window of Tolerance: The Polyvagal Theory by Stephen W. Porges Widen the Window by Dr. Elizabeth A. Stanley The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown Self-Compassion by Kristin Neff #Feedback #ReceivingFeedback #EmotionalIntelligence #WindowOfTolerance #NervousSystemRegulation #SelfAwareness #PersonalGrowth #PsychologicalSafety #CommunicationSkills #DefensiveBehavior #GrowthMindset #ProfessionalDevelopment #SelfCompassion #LeadershipDevelopment #EmotionalRegulation #ConstructiveCriticism #WorkplaceSkills #RelationshipSkills #FeedbackCulture #Coachability Written by Malynnda Stewart, PhD, BCPA A leading expert on difficult conversations. Her focus includes trauma-informed mediation, neuroscience-based feedback delivery, and health communication. your articles are always a godsend, they are helping me so much that nowadays I can actually feel my inner voice changing, in a good way :) Saving this to read up again. Such a nuanced and well worded and most importantly much needed article for today's times. There’s a specific kind of internal panic I feel when someone starts a sentence with I need to talk to you about something, and my brain immediately goes into a defensive fight or flight mode.

title: Healthy Boundaries Between You and Companies
author: Kike Peña
content_chunks: Healthy Boundaries Between You and Companies Companies are not absolute, and everything that happens within them can affect your profession. Press enter or click to view image in full size Companies are not evil, but to enter the corporate game, we need to know how to play it. It was 2006, and I remember calling my dad to have a serious conversation about quitting my first job. I was in my last design career semester and hungry to start making some money on my own, so I decided to get my first gig. I was 22, and I wanted to start living a real professional experience. I remember that call didn’t go well. To my dad, the idea of “resignating duties” was a sign of failure, even though I tried to explain that I was not happy there at all. He was a man of another era, and, like many of our parents, he spent his whole life working for the same company, so quitting was not an option. I learned a lot from that first work experience; the phone conversation with my father later made me reflect on my future expectations and professional learning. Unlike him, now we value other aspects of work that surely weren’t options in his day, such as culture, the quality of the people, and, above all, career paths and growth. My first work experience wasn’t rainbows and sunshine at all, as I mentioned. Being the TV advertising content assistant number 2, that is, the assistant to the assistant, wasn’t fun, and beyond the complex name and the two-color interface I needed to operate to include TV advertising commercials every day, there were valuable things I learned from that experience that today are wisdom gems I transmit when leading teams. Statements like: “What can be wrong if you are working at [enter company name]?” sound like my old man when he said: “In my days, working for ‘X’ meant prestige and progress.” Maybe he was right, but again, it was another time when seeking better conditions or a balance between personal life and work was unthinkable. My dad’s working life revolved around the importance of the company where he worked his whole life, and it is this concept that the company and its context were everything that I want to reflect on regarding small ideas that are more valuable today. For those starting a professional journey, as I did 20 years ago, this advice might help you see the work dynamics with fresh eyes. You are as good as the place you work at.

Yes, the company you work for or aspire to work for matters. My dad was right about that, and I still firmly believe it. There is a saying in Spanish that goes: “Dime con quien andas, y te diré quien eres.” In English, it will be something like: “A man is known by the company he keeps.” This message is 100% true: having the blessing of being part of a group of talented people with good prestige will encourage you to grow and seek excellence. In this scenario, whether you are starting a professional journey or thinking about changing your current job position, please note that a company’s reputation might say a lot about you or even play in your favor or against you. One of the good things about my first job was being part of the biggest media enterprise in my country; that was a big deal, and when I bragged to friends, they were like: “OMG, you are so lucky, I bet you are so happy to be there,” and I was, partially. Having a big company name behind you opens up many possibilities in the future, but it’s not just about a logo. Everything that happens within a company affects you one way or another; it is not only a matter of showing up, putting on headsets, and working. The way people talk or relate to each other, the way main events are presented to teammates, and how people face problems, among other things, will say a lot about the environment you are in. I’ve heard a lot of people saying they mind their own business and being careless of external matters, but that’s an illusion because, whether you want to or not, you are part of a collective. This first reflection is only an introduction to deeper topics, because it is not only about finding a proper place to develop your professional talent, or having a big name on your resume, but also about setting healthy boundaries between you and your work. So you found a nice place to work, people are amazing, and you feel you are making a difference with your work, it is so nice that one day you became “an ambassador” of the place you work. Every conversation you have, or every idea you post on social media, is about how great the company you work for is. Is this behavior a negative thing? Maybe not, but sometimes, or in my opinion, most of the time, it will backfire on you. Get Kike Peña’s stories in your inbox Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

“So now are you proposing to be disconnected from all the company communications and achievements?” — NO, I don’t recommend that either; actually, being part of a company culture is strategic because it sends a positive message to others that can eventually influence decisions about your progress or role. Believe me, I’ve been that disconnected person, and the results weren’t good for me. Reality is that in companies, there are “corporate subtleties” that, whether you like it or not, you have to learn to navigate. When the line between your identity and the company’s narrative blurs, the impact on your decisions about growth, new opportunities, and openness to new ideas is likely to be biased, I’ve seen how this perspective can be devastating for people when the very company they profess and brag about inevitably makes cold, calculated decisions about your future — decisions that are sometimes not so positive, which leads us to the next lesson. You are just a number, not the company’s soul. One day, you were called to an express meeting in an isolated room, received a strange email from HR, or suddenly, a meeting appeared in your calendar with a subject like this: “Let’s talk.” The so-called company you professed and praised is now letting you off. Why does this happen? It can be for numerous reasons, but independent of years, experience, personality, or achievements, no matter how much heart you put into this company, at the end of the day, you are always a number. Businesses are numbers, so it is not too complicated to calculate how decisions are made without your consent. The time to replace you is about 2 weeks, during which someone else will be doing the exact things you were doing, even cheaper. Is this wrong? No, it’s just reality, and how businesses work, so we should look at these dynamics with realistic eyes. A “family” is not the correct label. Be careful when someone inside the company starts to proclaim this idea. I don’t even go into explaining why this statement is the most dangerous concept in companies. Believing the people who work around you are as relevant as your parents, kids, or partner puts you in an unfair position. As I said, no matter how much heart you put into “this relationship,” one day the numbers will come up in the conversation, and nothing else will matter. Choose yourself over a company. It is common to feel “ungrateful” when you decide to quit your position or decline a job offer. Why? Sometimes people (myself included) still have this old sense of loyalty to those in power, as we are always in debt. Still, as I mentioned above, we are talking about a business relationship in which we need to put ourselves first. It will never be a scenario where putting the company values first over emotional stability or family is good.

I deeply believe we are in companies to fulfill a mission (for which we are paid), to play a role, and to achieve results. Those concepts are valuable for a period of time; you may either evolve into a new position or take on another professional challenge in some other place, but it’s not a forever thing. There’s no need to feel bad when you choose to grow in a certain period of time. Remember that, one way or another, companies will make a money decision over you, with no remorse or feelings, just transactional operations. As time passes, it will be great to have positive memories of your achievements and adventures rather than end your days saying you gave your best life moments to a corporation. Work is a transactional relationship; it is something you get paid for without expecting good relationships or extreme commitment from your side. The formula is simple: you are good at something, a company pays for your knowledge or execution, and that’s it. No one is expecting to find the love of their life (it can happen), solid friendships (it can also happen), but the core of this interaction is money and nothing more. Companies are not evil, but to enter the corporate game, we need to know how to play it. This advice can be the most relevant wisdom you shall pass to your young team; they are hungry to grow and make a difference, but do not let them be blind to the rules of the long-term corporate game. My first work experience wasn’t rainbows and sunshine at all, as I mentioned. The first one usually never is. Working at a respected company can open doors. FIJ FDREi Hi GTTF NBLOI CGUIEWTUT
